segments.s190-g41: عنان را is the direct object (عنان "reins") plus the object marker را; the clitic marks the reins as definite/accusative. بپیچید is a 3rd‑person past verb (archaic/epic form of پیچیدن) meaning “(he) turned/whipped/twisted,” and it follows the object here. سوی فرود is a prepositional phrase (سوی “toward” + فرود “descent/low ground”) indicating the direction of the turning.
segments.s190-g42: دلش and سرش are nouns with the 3rd‑person possessive clitic ش (his): “his heart” and “his head.” پر ز + noun is a poetic adjectival construction meaning “full of (X)” (پر “full” + ز = از). There is no copula; nominal phrases (دلش پر ز کین و سرش پر ز دود) describe his state in parallel, with و linking them. کین is an archaic/epic word for vengeance/hatred and دود means smoke (literal or metaphorical).
segments.s190-g1: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' placed before گفت (past 3rd‑person 'said'). پس is a temporal adverb 'then'. پهلوان is the subject 'the champion/hero'. با + proper name (با زرسپ) here functions dative/ethical — 'to Zarasp' or 'addressing Zarasp' rather than literal accompaniment. The verb گفت precedes the full subject phrase because of poetic inversion.
segments.s190-g2: که introduces the reported content (a subordinate clause, roughly 'that' or beginning the quoted command). بَفروز is an optative/imperative form 'kindle/set afire' (ب‑ prefix common in classical Persian imperatives/subjunctives). دل را is a definite direct object with را after دل. چو is a comparative 'like/as', and آذرگشسپ is the standard proper name used as the comparison model.
segments.s190-g3: This is an imperative clause: سلیح ('arms, weaponry') appears before its recipient 'سواران جنگی' ('the war‑riders'), and the verb بپوش (imperative 'put on/arm') comes finally — a common poetic order where the object precedes the command verb. جنگی qualifies سواران as 'warlike' or 'military riders.'
segments.s190-g4: بجان is the prepositional/poetic ب + جان meaning roughly 'for/by (your) life' or 'take care of your life'; و تن joins 'and body.' خویشتن‌دار is an adjectival/imperative sense 'self‑controlled, restrained' (literally 'hold yourself'), and گوش (literally 'ear') here functions idiomatically as 'heed/listen' or 'be attentive.' The verb 'be' is omitted (elliptical), so the line reads as commands/admonitions.
segments.s190-g5: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you.' خواهی is present/future or an interrogative 'do you wish/want' (2nd‑person). مگر here functions as a modal/interrogative particle 'perhaps'/'is it that' inserted after the verb. کین 'vengeance' is the object, modified by آن نامدار 'that famed one.' Word order places the object phrase after the verb with the particle for emphasis — typical poetic syntax.
segments.s190-g6: وگرنه 'otherwise' begins the conditional/contrast. نبینم is the 1st‑person negative present/subjunctive form 'I do not see / I cannot see' (the negative prefix ن‑ attaches to the present form). کسی here, under the negation, functions as 'no one' (negated indefinite), and خواستار is a predicate adjective 'desirous / seeking.' The clause means 'otherwise I see no one who desires (it).'
segments.s190-g7: زرسپ آمد is a simple past clause 'Zarasp came.' The conjunction و links to the next action. ترگ بر سر نهاد is a past transitive construction: ترگ 'a rag' (object), بر سر 'upon (the) head' (prepositional phrase), and نهاد is the past tense 'placed/put' (the third sense listed). The object precedes the verb in normal Persian word order.
segments.s190-g8: This is a parallel descriptive nominal phrase: دلی پر ز کین = 'a heart full of kīn (vengeance),' where دلی stands for 'heart' and پر ز = 'full of.' The second half parallels it: لبی پر ز باد = 'a lip full of wind,' where باد literally 'wind' carries the figurative sense of empty talk/boasting. There is no finite verb here; these are verbless attributes describing Zarasp.
segments.s190-g9: خروشان is an adverbial participle meaning “roaring” or “with a roar,” placed frontally to set the scene. باسپ here is a poetic contraction of باِ اسپ (با + اسپ) = “with (his) horse,” followed by اندر آورد پای, where اندر (in/into) + آورد (past of آوردن, “brought/drew”) govern پای (“foot/hoof”); the verb phrase literally ‘brought/drew the foot in.’ Word order is typical of older Persian where adverbials and prepositional clusters precede the verb.
segments.s190-g10: بکردار is an epithet modifying the participant whose ‘fire’ follows (literally “of good/valiantly‑deed / well‑behaved” here functioning as ‘brave’). آتش درآمد ز جای is a common idiom: آتش (fire) + درآمد (came forth/appeared) + ز (from) + جای (place) — ‘the fire sprang up from its place.’ درآمد is used as an intransitive verbal noun/verb meaning ‘appeared/issued forth’ rather than ‘income.’
segments.s190-g11: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening meaning “thus said” or “he thus spoke.” شیر ژیان is a name or title (literally ‘lion‑ژیان’), functioning as the speaker; با introduces the addressee (با تخوار = ‘to/with Takhvar’). In older Persian سخن patterns the verb گفت precedes or stands with the speaker phrase as here.
segments.s190-g12: که introduces a subordinate content clause (‘that’). آمد is a past tense verb ‘came’ or ‘arrived’; دگرگون is an adjective/participial modifier ‘changed/transformed’; یکی نامدار is a nominal phrase ‘a certain renowned one’ (یکی = ‘one/a certain’, نامدار = ‘famous/renowned’). The clause reads literally ‘that a renowned one came transformed.’
segments.s190-g13: ببین is an imperative ‘look/see.’ تا here introduces purpose/result (‘so that’) and is followed by شناسی, which in context functions as a subjunctive/optative form ‘you may know’ (from شناختن) — the dictionary head gives noun senses, but in the line it is verbal. که این مرد کیست is an embedded interrogative clause ‘who this man is’ (که = that/introducing clause; کی = who; ست = copula ‘is’).
segments.s190-g14: یکی شهریار است: یکی (‘one/a certain’) + شهریار (‘king/ruler’) + است (copula ‘is’) — ‘he is a certain ruler.’ اگر here functions in the older sense ‘or/whether’ introducing the alternative لشکریست, where لشکریست is an adjectival/predicative form meaning ‘military/commanding troops’ or ‘a warrior/one with an army.’ Thus the sentence offers two possible identities.
segments.s190-g15: چنین گفت again ‘thus spoke’; with + addressee is marked by با (here ‘with/to’). شاه جنگی is a nominal phrase where جنگی is an epithet ‘warlike/military,’ so با شاه جنگی تخوار reads as ‘Takhvar spoke to/with the warlike king’ (Takhvar being the speaker or participant). Word order places the adverbial formula before the recipient phrase.
segments.s190-g16: که introduces the content clause (‘that’). آمد is past ‘came’/‘arrived’; گه here means ‘occasion/time’ (the coming of a time); گردش روزگار is a fixed phrase ‘the turning/rotation of the times,’ i.e. the turn of fortune. The clause therefore says ‘that the occasion/turning of fate has come.’
segments.s190-g25: بداند is a third‑person singular past/present narrative form of دانستن (‘to know’) put first for poetic word order; it governs the following noun phrase سپهدار دیوانه طوس, where سپهدار (“commander”) is the title, دیوانه an epithet (“madman”), and طوس the proper name in apposition. The whole phrase is the subject of بداند (i.e. “Tūs the commander, the madman, knows”).
segments.s190-g26: که is the subordinate/complementizer introducing the content clause. ایدر means “here”; نبودیم is the 1st‑person plural past negative of بودن (‘be’), literally “we were not”; ما is the explicit pronoun ‘we’ placed after the verb for emphasis (poetic inversion). بر + فسوس marks the relation to the noun فسوس (“insidious whisper/idle talk”) – here meaning something like “to be subject to / taken in by whisperings.”
segments.s190-g27: فرود here functions as a noun/adverbial ‘alighting / dismounting’ placed before the clause; دلاور is the subject ‘the brave one/hero’; برانگیخت is the past tense verb ‘roused / stirred (up)’ and اسپ is the object ‘steed/horse’. Poetic word order places the adverbial and the subject before the verb and leaves the object after the verb.
segments.s190-g28: یکی functions as an indefinite subject ‘someone’; تیر + زد is a common collocation ‘shot an arrow / struck with an arrow’ with زد in the simple past third person. بر میان marks the target location ‘into the middle (of…)’, and زرسپ is the proper name of the target (Zarasp). The prepositional phrase follows the verb for emphasis in this poetic line.
segments.s190-g29: که introduces a result/explanatory clause (‘so that/that’). با is the instrumental preposition ‘by/with’; کوهه + زین is a genitive compound meaning ‘the mount’s (or horse’s) saddle’ (poetic ordering of modifiers); تنش را is the definite object (‘his body’) with the object marker را. بدوخت is the past tense ‘sewed’ (here used figuratively: the body is said to be ‘sewn’ to the saddle—i.e. pinned/held fast).
segments.s190-g30: روانش (‘his soul / spirit’) is the possessed noun (‑ش = his). ز is the poetic preposition ‘from / by’; پیکان او is ‘the arrow of him/that man’ (او = the shooter); برفروخت is a figurative past verb here (‘was kindled / inflamed’), used poetically for the soul’s being struck/fading—i.e. a poetic way to report the mortal wound/effect of the arrow.
segments.s190-g31: بیفتاد is past third person ‘he fell’; و connects to برگشت ‘returned/drew back’. ازو is the prepositional object ‘from him’; بادپای is a descriptive epithet or name meaning ‘the swift‑footed (one)’ and functions as the actor of برگشت in poetic inversion (the phrase order is shifted for meter and emphasis).
segments.s190-g32: همی شد is a small narrative phrase ‘thus/so it happened’ (همی is emphatic/iterative), دمان و دنان are two coordinated nouns (literally ‘hem’/‘skirt’ and a poetic/archaic term دِنان) that form the grammatical subject(s), and باز جای means ‘again in place’ or ‘restored to their places’. The clause states that these items were set back into position.
segments.s190-g33: خروشی (a noun) + برآمد (past tense verb) form the core: a sound/cry 'arose'. ز ایران سپاه is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from the army of Iran' (word order is inverted compared with modern spoken Persian; سپاه governs the whole phrase).
segments.s190-g34: زسر (a prepositional compound 'from the head') appears before the verb برگرفتند (past, 3rd-person plural 'they lifted/took up'), and گردان کلاه is the object: 'helmets of the troop' or 'the troop's helmets'. The clause thus means that they lifted (took off/raised) their helmets from their heads; word order (object after verb) is standard in Ferdowsi's epic style.
segments.s190-g35: دل طوس is 'the heart of Tūs' (possessor follows the noun without an explicit ezāfe, a common archaic/poetic ordering). پرخون and پراب are adjectival compounds meaning 'full of blood' and 'full of water/tears' respectively, linked by و 'and'.
segments.s190-g36: بپوشید is a past (3rd-singular) narrative form 'he put on' (the initial ب- is an older/periphrastic/preverbal element common in epic diction). جوشن is the direct object 'armor/ cuirass'. هم 'also' modifies the verb, and اندر شتاب is an adverbial phrase 'in haste'.
segments.s190-g37: ز + گردان جنگی = 'from the war-troop/division'. بنالید is past (3rd-singular) 'he/it wailed/moaned'. سخت is an adverb here 'fiercely/strongly' modifying بنالید.
segments.s190-g38: بلرزید is past (3rd-singular) 'it/they trembled' or 'it shook'. برسان followed by برگ درخت functions idiomatically to indicate extent/result — 'to the point of (reaching) the leaves of the tree' — i.e., the trembling extended as far as the tree's leaves. This is a poetic use derived from رساندن 'to bring/reach'.
segments.s190-g39: نشست is past tense 'he sat down' or 'he alighted'; از بر زین = 'from upon the saddle' (از + بر + زین together indicate dismounting). چو کوهی بزرگ is a simile 'like a great mountain' modifying the manner of his alighting (poetic comparison).
segments.s190-g40: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. بنهند is a present-subjunctive/optative 3rd-person-plural of نَهَادن (here used as a narrative present meaning 'they place/set'), governing the following prepositional phrase بر پشت پیلی سترگ 'upon the back of a mighty elephant'. The clause describes the mountain-like bulk as if it were set on an elephant's back.
segments.s190-g17: که here introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('that' / 'who'). این پور طوسست is an old poetic predicate: پور = 'son' and طوسست is copulative/relational (='of Tūs' / 'belonging to Tūs'), with the copula fused to the name. نامش = 'his name' (نام + enclitic ش), followed by the name زرسپ in apposition; in poetry the verb 'is' is omitted, so 'نامش زرسپ' reads 'his name (is) Zarasp.'
segments.s190-g18: که again introduces a relative clause describing Zarasp. از + پیل جنگی marks the adversary/thing he faces ('from/before a war‑elephant'); جنگی is a war‑forming adjective modifying پیل. نگرداند is negative (ن + گرداند) with گرداند 'to turn / cause to turn'; here it means 'does not make (the) horse turn away' (i.e. he does not falter before an elephant). اسپ is the direct object 'steed/horse'. The clause uses an older word order where the verb precedes its object (poetic inversion).
segments.s190-g19: که introduces another descriptive clause. جفتست is a predicative adjective/verb meaning 'is paired/matched' (older form with the copula fused). با خواهر indicates the partner ('with the sister'), and ریونیز is the proper name of that sister. The order is subject‑predicate with the predication expressed by جفتست followed by the complement introduced by با.
segments.s190-g20: بکین is a poetic/archaic verbal form (variant of بکن, 'to do' / sometimes 'to take vengeance'); here it functions with آمدست to indicate purpose/result: 'has come to (do / act)'. آمدست is an older perfect/past form 'has come'. The phrase ends with این جهانجوی نیز (this world‑seeker/ambitious man, also), so the verb phrase appears before its subject for poetic word order; نیز = 'also'.
segments.s190-g21: چو بیند is a temporal clause ('when he sees'); چو = 'when' and بیند = 'he sees' (classical present/3rd sg). The following series بر و بازوی و مغفرت lists the things seen: بر (here a protective item/armour or 'shield/defense'), بازوی = 'arm', مغفرت is an item of protective gear (in context an armour/covering). The coordinated nouns form the object of بیند; in poetic diction the verb comes early and the objects follow linked by و.
segments.s190-g22: خدنگی (a dagger) is the (implicit) weapon; بباید is a modal auxiliary 'must/ought' (present/3rd sg), and گشاد is an archaic verbal/adjectival form related to 'open/draw' (here 'unsheathe/draw'). از برت literally 'from your side/on you' (از + بر + enclitic ت indicating 'from your side'). Together the clause says he must draw/unsheathe a dagger from your side; note the modal + participle structure and the prepositional source phrase 'از برت.'
segments.s190-g23: بدان is an imperative formed from the prefix ب‑ + دان ('know') = 'know this' (command). تا introduces a subordinating/time clause 'until', and بخاک اندر آید is 'goes into the earth' (بخاک = 'to the ground', اندر آید = 'comes inside' / 'sinks in'). سرش = 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش). The verb آید follows the locative phrase, reflecting poetic inversion: 'until his head comes into the earth.'
segments.s190-g24: نگون اندر آید = 'is/shall be overthrown / cast down' (نگون = overthrown / downfallen, plus اندر آید 'comes in/comes down'). ز باره برش gives the cause: ز = 'by/from', باره = 'burden/weight (or blow/occasion)', and برش (here a fused form) indicates 'upon him' (poetic compressed expression). The whole phrase means he will be cast down by the burden/blow laid upon him.

sentences.s190-s21: He turned the reins toward the descent; his heart full of hatred and his head full of smoke.
sentences.s190-s1: Thus the champion then said to Zarasp: "Set the heart ablaze like Azar‑Gashasp."
sentences.s190-s2: Arm the war‑riders with weapons; guard your life and body, be self‑restrained and attentive.
sentences.s190-s3: Do you want, perhaps, vengeance against that famed man? Otherwise I see no one who desires it.
sentences.s190-s4: Zarasp came and placed a rag upon his head — a heart full of hatred and a lip full of wind.
sentences.s190-s5: Roaring, with his horse he drew up a foot; the fire of the brave leapt up from its place.
sentences.s190-s6: Thus said Shir Zhiyan to Takhvar: a renowned man has come, changed.
sentences.s190-s7: Look, so that you may know who this man is; he is either a ruler or a warrior.
sentences.s190-s8: Thus Takhvar spoke with the warlike king: the turn of fortune has come.
sentences.s190-s13: Tūs, the commander and madman, knows that we were not here to be taken in by whisperings.
sentences.s190-s14: The brave man dismounted and roused his steed; someone shot an arrow into Zarasp’s middle.
sentences.s190-s15: So that, by the mount’s saddle, his body was sewn to it; his soul was kindled by that (other) arrow.
sentences.s190-s16: He fell and the swift‑footed drew back from him; then the hem and the dānān were again restored to their place.
sentences.s190-s17: A shout rose from the Iranian host; the troops lifted their helmets off their heads.
sentences.s190-s18: Tūs's heart was full of blood and his eyes full of water; he donned his cuirass as well, in haste.
sentences.s190-s19: From the war-host a loud lament went up; it trembled so that even the leaves of the tree shook.
sentences.s190-s20: He alighted from the saddle like a great mountain, which they place upon the back of a mighty elephant.
sentences.s190-s9: That this is Tūs's son — his name is Zarasp — who will not turn his steed away from a war‑elephant.
sentences.s190-s10: That he is matched with the sister Rīvonīz; this ambitious warrior has also come.
sentences.s190-s11: When he sees your shield and arm and cuirass, he will have to draw a dagger against you.
sentences.s190-s12: Know this: until his head goes down into the earth, he will be cast down by the weight laid upon him.
